editorialContract.textPurpose: character reaction to a museum exhibit
editorialContract.verbosity: deliberately varied from micro-reactions to compact performances
editorialContract.chronology: publication order is canonical; callbacks may only point backward
editorialContract.assignmentPolicy: preserve reviewer, rating, Reaction, publisher, Component and source identity

continuityNotes.note: Grief answers Captain Reiko Vane on the same butterfly-back exhibit.
The Keeper reframes the Griffin’s preset claim on the same butterfly-front exhibit.

author.name: The Daisy Who Saw It Start

revisions.editedComment: The number changed again. I wrote it down in red where the council can see. If an admin presses Sync, the garden may grow while we are still counting.
The count rises because the road is still being built beneath our feet. Mark the distance, little daisy. A purpose is easier to loosen when the milestones are honest.
Folders first, then the dishes inside; one clear button returns the visitor to the menu. Good service. PuzzleBot, labels are not garnish and lunch is not an escape room.
Folder. Component. Back. Solved.
Sender on the left, Bot on the right, the whole thread counted between them, and a reply box ready when you are, bubbulah. Delete can wait until after the prophecy.
The spread shows who spoke, who answered, what remains in the thread, and one card marked Continue. Delete lies face down. You are still reading. Good—that changes the outcome.
Five stars hover above a named reaction, an optional comment, Clear, Submit, perhaps Share. You came to judge the exhibit; the card quietly records how you judged yourself.
Three stars. CLAPPED. Submitted. Shift continues.
Top wing. Bottom wing. Size and rotation holding. Name and message visible. Safety stays on; this display is for choosing how the butterfly flies, not whether it deserves to.
Captain, beneath ‘settings confirmed’ I hear relief that the wings kept moving. We can leave the weapon out of the report. Choose the colors slowly; nothing here is hunting you.
Size, speed, wingbeat, rotation, starting ground, colors, behavior—every useful claim above the dirt. I will take Flock and half the horizon. The mirrors may keep Mouse.
Quiet near the glass. Random, complementary, analogous, same, primary; float, flock, follow, clear. The Griffin calls them presets. I call them doors that open without losing the creatures.
I paid for one wandering butterfly: colors chosen at arrival, a path made from gentle noise, always turning before the edge. No receipt. Let the kindness keep moving.
The butterfly picked its own colors, crossed the store, turned at the boundary, and changed size with distance. This is apparently normal inventory behavior. Gary logged the shift.
The usual, please: one label, one range, one chosen value. Move it a little. Small controls survive because someone returns and still knows what they are for.
Little line. Little number. Moves when asked, stops where chosen. The Last Customer comes back. I sit beside the slider and try not to break the harbor. Hello.
A new Bot begins like a cutting: name, subtitle, purpose, type, theme, designer, avatar. Keep the healthy fields; let the AI regrow only what needs pruning. Then Save.
Name, vibe, avatar, AI upgrade checkboxes—finally, a laboratory with a Seed button and pies clearly labeled for accessibility. *labels pie ‘DO NOT UPDATE KEPT FIELDS’*
Found my reward in the gallery. Rarity filter above, Current Reward below, Add and Edit nearby. Bounty still wrong. Wind says Clear; I say we were never here.
Gallery’s got rarity filters, a selected reward, Add, Edit, and Clear. Dust-boy says the wrong bounty makes him taller. Death lowers a man’s standards and improves his posture.
